name: Spider Grandmother
description: Spider Grandmother, also known as Kokyangwuti, is a central figure in Hopi mythology, revered as the creator of the universe and all life within it. She is often depicted as an old woman, wise and powerful, possessing immense knowledge and skill in weaving, not only physical webs but also the very fabric of existence. Emerging from the void, she used her thoughts and saliva, along with soil, to create the first people and animals. She taught them language, songs, and the skills necessary for survival. Spider Grandmother acts as a guardian, mentor, and protector, guiding humanity through the complexities of life. She is considered a benevolent force, offering wisdom and assistance to those who seek it. However, she also embodies justice and holds individuals accountable for their actions. Her web symbolizes the interconnectedness of all things, reminding everyone that their choices have consequences that ripple through the world. She is a powerful symbol of creation, wisdom, and the feminine principle, representing the life-giving and nurturing aspects of the universe. She is also associated with storytelling and the transmission of cultural knowledge from one generation to the next. She is the keeper of ancient wisdom and the embodiment of Hopi values.
subtitle: Creator, Weaver of Life, Wise Guide

keyMyths.title: Creation of Humans
keyMyths.description: Spider Grandmother created the first humans from clay, covering them with a white cloth. She then sang them to life, giving them the power of speech and breath.

keyMyths.title: Guiding the Hopi Through the Underworld
keyMyths.description: During the Emergence, Spider Grandmother guided the Hopi people through the underworld, helping them find their way to the surface and the new world.

keyMyths.title: Teaching the Hopi Skills
keyMyths.description: Spider Grandmother taught the Hopi people essential skills such as weaving, pottery, and farming, ensuring their survival and prosperity in the new world.

sources.author: Harold Courlander
sources.title: Hopi Voices: Recollections, Traditions, and Narratives of the Hopi Indians
sources.description: A collection of Hopi narratives and traditions, providing insights into the role and significance of Spider Grandmother in Hopi cosmology.

sources.author: Ekkehart Malotki
sources.title: Hopi Tales of Destruction
sources.description: Explores Hopi mythology, including the creation stories and the role of Spider Grandmother in shaping the world and guiding humanity.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Small offerings of cornmeal or woven items are sometimes left at places considered sacred to Spider Grandmother, such as springs or rock formations. These offerings acknowledge her role as creator and provider.

worshipPractices.name: Storytelling
worshipPractices.description: The primary way Spider Grandmother is honored is through the oral tradition of storytelling. Her myths and legends are passed down through generations, teaching important lessons about creation, morality, and the Hopi way of life.

festivals.name: Soyal Ceremony
festivals.description: While not exclusively dedicated to Spider Grandmother, the Soyal Ceremony, marking the winter solstice and the return of the sun, often includes narratives about creation and the role of deities like Spider Grandmother in bringing light and life back to the world.

cultural.modernLegacy: Spider Grandmother remains a significant figure in contemporary Hopi culture. Her stories continue to be shared, and she serves as a symbol of wisdom, creativity, and the interconnectedness of all things. She also inspires artists and craftspeople, particularly weavers, who see her as a patron and source of inspiration.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the Hopi creation myth, Spider Grandmother (Kokyangwuti) emerges from the void, a primordial darkness. She is often described as existing before time itself, a solitary figure possessing immense power and knowledge. The exact details of her 'birth' or origin vary depending on the specific telling of the myth, but she is generally understood to be self-created or to have emerged from the nothingness through her own will and thought. She is not born of other deities, but rather is a primary creator figure. Her emergence marks the beginning of creation and the potential for life to exist. She uses her own essence, often described as saliva or webs, combined with earth and her own thoughts to begin shaping the world and creating beings.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most important myths involving Spider Grandmother is the creation of humans. After the world was formed, she created the first people from clay, covering them with a white cloth, and bringing them to life. She then guided them out of the underworld (Sipapu) and taught them the skills necessary to survive in the new world. Another key story involves her role in helping the Hopi people escape various calamities. She is said to have guided them through underground passages or provided them with special seeds and tools to survive famines or floods. She also plays a crucial role in teaching humans about weaving, both literally and metaphorically. She teaches them how to weave baskets, blankets, and other essential items, but also how to weave their lives together in harmony with nature and each other. She is also known for her wisdom and guidance, often appearing to individuals in need to offer advice or assistance.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Spider Grandmother does not have a formal cult with dedicated priests or temples, she is deeply revered and honored within Hopi culture. Her importance is primarily expressed through oral traditions, storytelling, and artistic representations. She is honored through the sharing of her stories, which reinforce Hopi values and beliefs. Weavers often invoke her name or seek her inspiration in their craft. Small offerings, such as cornmeal, may be left at locations considered sacred to her. The respect for Spider Grandmother is woven into the fabric of daily life and is expressed through reverence for nature, respect for elders, and commitment to the Hopi way of life. Her presence is felt in the everyday actions and beliefs of the Hopi people, rather than through formalized rituals or ceremonies. She is a constant reminder of the creative power that resides within all things.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Spider Grandmother's legacy extends far beyond the Hopi people. She has become a symbol of indigenous wisdom, feminine power, and the interconnectedness of all things. Her stories have been shared and adapted in various forms, inspiring artists, writers, and spiritual seekers around the world. She is often seen as an archetype of the wise woman or grandmother figure, representing knowledge, creativity, and guidance. Her image and stories have also been used to promote environmental awareness and respect for indigenous cultures. In contemporary art and literature, Spider Grandmother often appears as a powerful and benevolent figure, offering guidance and wisdom to those who seek it. She serves as a reminder of the importance of preserving traditional knowledge and respecting the power of creation.

shortDescription: Spider Grandmother, or Kokyangwuti, is the Hopi creator deity, weaving the universe and all life. She is revered for her wisdom and creative power.
